design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore haunted mansion, solve environmental puzzles, manage limited inventory, avoid/defeat supernatural enemies to escape
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (inventory weight limits, limited weapons) combined with supernatural threat and uncertainty about which enemies can be fought vs. avoided vs. require puzzle solutions
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Survive investigation of a cursed mansion through cunning, exploration, and adaptive problem-solving rather than pure combat prowess
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Inventory is weight-based not slot-based, forcing meaningful tactical decisions. Not all enemies can be defeated directly—some require puzzle solutions, others must be avoided entirely. Discarded items persist in world, enabling backtracking strategy. Fixed camera angles frame 3D characters dramatically, emphasizing cinematic horror atmosphere over player control

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can choose combat vs. avoidance vs. puzzle-solving approaches to encounters. Inventory management creates self-imposed resource constraints. Ability to discard and retrieve items enables flexible strategy

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon collection (found throughout mansion, not earned)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Puzzle completion gates access to new floors: attic/third floor initially linear, completion unlocks non-linear access to first/second floors, final puzzle opens caverns beneath mansion
replayability_hooks: Choice between two protagonists (Edward Carnby or Emily Hartwood) with different motivations
Partially non-linear mid-game allows different room exploration sequences
Multiple solutions to encounters (combat, evasion, puzzle-solving)
Persistent item placement enables experimentation with discarding/retrieving strategy

world_building_techniques: Environmental texts and documents scattered throughout mansion explaining occult history
Character backstory revealed through exploration (Jeremy Hartwood's suicide mystery)
Setting-based lore delivery: Derceto mansion, built by occultist pirate Ezechiel Pregzt, contains caverns used for dark rituals
Supernatural antagonist motivation established through discovered documents rather than exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Varied encounter design philosophy: some enemies require direct combat, others demand puzzle solutions, still others cannot be defeated and must be avoided entirely. This prevents player from settling into single tactical approach
notable_bosses: Ezechiel Pregzt (occultist pirate spirit inhabiting tree in caverns—primary antagonist)

faction_indicators: Jeremy Hartwood (suicide victim, potential unwilling Pregzt host)
Edward Carnby (private investigator protagonist seeking piano)
Emily Hartwood (niece seeking truth about suicide)
Ezechiel Pregzt (antagonist occultist whose spirit persists)
danger_signals: Derceto mansion widely reputed as haunted
Doors mysteriously slam shut, trapping player
Supernatural monsters attack in attic upon entry
Jeremy Hartwood's suspicious suicide to prevent possession
Dark rituals performed in caverns for life extension and fortune magic
environmental_storytelling: Mansion architecture reflects occult history (caverns beneath used for rituals)
Burned/damaged sections indicate American Civil War incident
Old tree in caverns as physical manifestation of Pregzt's spirit
Documents hidden throughout house revealing backstory
Player must physically traverse the same path victim took, reinforcing narrative connection

sources_used: Wikipedia: Alone in the Dark (1992 video game)
